context: According to a study conducted by the Pew Research Institute, in 2019 73% of people over the age of 65 were connected to the internet

Jakob Nielsen

September 11, 2011

Share

Summary:  Users often leave Web pages in 10–20 seconds, but pages with a clear value proposition can hold people's attention for much longer
source_title: How Long Do Users Stay on Web Pages?

context: 99.9% of readers will now ask: What's a Weibull distribution
source_title: How Long Do Users Stay on Web Pages?

context:
Negative Aging: Leave Quick or Stay Long

The researchers discovered that 99% of web pages have a negative aging effect
source_title: How Long Do Users Stay on Web Pages?

context:
Jakob Nielsen

May 5, 2008

Share

Summary:  On the average Web page, users have time to read at most 28% of the words during an average visit; 20% is more likely

(In research for the book Prioritizing Web Usability, we found that people read only about 10% of the text that they supposedly "agreed" to.)

The following chart shows the average time users spend on pages with different word counts:

Obviously, users tend to spend more time on pages with more information
Thus, when you add verbiage to a page, you can assume that customers will read 18% of it
So, on average, users will have time to read 28% of the words if they devote all of their time to reading
More realistically, users will read about 20% of the text on the average page
However, the information above the fold still received most attention: even as recently as 2010, our eyetracking studies showed that 80% of users’ viewing time was spent above the fold
On general websites, more than 42% of the viewing time fell within the top 20% of the page, and more than 65% of the time was spent in the top 40% of the page
On general websites, more than 42% of the viewing time fell within the top 20% of the page, and more than 65% of the time was spent in the top 40% of the page
More than 65% of the viewing time above the fold was concentrated in the top half of the viewport

Scanning & Reading Patterns

We’ve seen that the content above the fold received most attention (57% of viewing time); the second screenful of content received about a third of that (17% viewing time); the remaining 26% was spread in a long-tail distribution
About 95% of the time, this fixed size is tiny , reducing readability significantly for most people over the age of 40
